rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical four-component 1,2-difunctionalization of arylalkenes with carboxylic acids, acetonitrile, and alcohols to form functionalized imides via a cascade Mumm rearrangement
electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of styrene generates a radical cation; cathodic reduction of methanol (and of PhCOOH) generates methoxide (and benzoate) nucleophiles plus H2; a second anodic oxidation converts the alpha-methoxy benzyl radical to a benzyl cation

key_intermediates.name: nitrilium ion / Ritter-type cation from CH3CN trapping (D)

electron_flow: At the anode, styrene is oxidized by SET to a radical cation, which is trapped by methoxide (generated by cathodic reduction of MeOH) to give a C-radical; a second anodic SET produces a benzyl cation that is intercepted by CH3CN (Ritter) to form a nitrilium; capture by cathodically generated benzoate gives an O-acyl isoamide that undergoes intramolecular Mumm rearrangement to the imide.
rate_determining_step: Neither MeO incorporation nor CH3CN incorporation is rate-determining (parallel CH3OH/CD3OD and CH3CN/CD3CN experiments both gave 1:1 product ratios); likely the initial anodic oxidation of styrene

probe_reagents.name: CD3OD (vs CH3OH)
probe_reagents.observation: Parallel CH3OH/CD3OD reaction gave 3ao/d3-3ao = 1:1 (84% combined), indicating MeO incorporation is not rate-determining

probe_reagents.name: CD3CN (vs CH3CN)
probe_reagents.observation: Parallel CH3CN/CD3CN reaction gave 3ao/d3-3ao = 1:1 (82% combined), indicating CH3CN incorporation is not rate-determining

probe_reagents.name: [18O]-4-cyanobenzoic acid
probe_reagents.observation: 18O is incorporated into the imide carbonyl of [18O]-3ak, confirming the acid oxygen ends up in the imide and supporting a Mumm rearrangement from an O-acyl isoamide

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: Styrene has the lowest oxidation potential (1.60 V vs SCE) versus MeOH and benzoic acid (no peak 0-2.5 V), supporting initial SET oxidation of styrene

notes: The paper highlights generation of an unstable O-acyl isoamide (E) as a rare in situ precursor for a 1,3-(O→N) acyl Mumm rearrangement. Authors compare against alternative literature electrochemical alkene difunctionalization manifolds (Scheme 1, paths a-f: external-oxidant radical addition, direct anodic oxidation of substrate vs alkene, and mediator-based variants); the favored pathway here is direct anodic oxidation of the alkene to the radical cation (path d-type) coupled with cathodic generation of nucleophiles (paired electrolysis).
